Behavior: Neurological Syndromes
Assess functional role of visual areas by examining
behavior in patients with lesions.
Necessity approach.

Behavior: Neurological Syndromes
Akinetopsia
Impaired motion perception due to cortical damage.
"Motion" becomes
an inferred process.

Behavior: Neurological Syndromes
Akinetopsia
Impaired motion perception due to cortical damage.
Achromatopsia
Impaired color perception due to cortical damage.
Distinct from peripheral color blindness due to
absence of certain photoreceptors.

Behavior: Neurological Syndromes
Akinetopsia
Impaired motion perception due to cortical damage.
Achromatopsia
Impaired color perception due to cortical damage.
Distinct from peripheral color blindness due to
absence of certain photoreceptors.

Behavior: Neurological Syndromes
Akinetopsia
Impaired motion perception due to cortical damage.
Achromatopsia
Impaired color perception due to cortical damage.
Distinct from peripheral color blindness due to
absence of certain photoreceptors.

Hemiachromatopsia: Unilateral lesion
Preserved shape
knowledge (drawings)

Bilateral achromatopsia:
Oliver Sack's case of the color-blind painter, Mr. I.
Letter from patient to Sacks:
"I am a rather successful artist just past 65 years of age.
I was driving my car and was hit by a small truck on the passenger side
of my vehicle ... While taking an eye examination, it was discovered that
I was unable to distinguish letters or colors. My vision was such that
everything appeared to me as viewing a black and white television
screen.
Within days, I could distinguish letters and my vision became that of an
eagle-I can see a worm wriggling a block away. The sharpness of focus
is incredible.
BUT-I AM ABSOLUTELY COLOR BLIND."

Experience of visual world can be drastically altered.
Oliver Sack's case of the color-blind painter, Mr. I.
Found foods disgusting due to grayish, dead appearance
-- closing eyes didn't help because image remained grey
Humans no longer looked attractive
-- "like animated gray statues"
-- including his own image in mirror
-- avoided social situations
-- repulsed by sex; flesh looked "rat-colored"
Color knowledge remained extraordinary
-- could name color of Van Gogh's billiard table
and the walls
-- But unable to see with eyes or imagination
